title: Hiring Your Spouse in Family Business

requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must legitimately employ spouse in family business to provide tax-deductible employee benefits, establish qualified retirement plans, and optimize household income allocation while maintaining proper employment relationship
criteria: Spouse must perform bona fide services for business with reasonable compensation for work performed
Document employment relationship with job description, time records, and actual duties performed
Pay reasonable compensation comparable to what would be paid to unrelated employee for same work
Spouse employment enables medical reimbursement plan, retirement plan, and other employee benefit deductions
If business is sole proprietorship employing only spouse, can elect out of FICA taxes (both must elect)
Must issue W-2 to spouse and follow proper payroll tax withholding and reporting procedures
Consider qualified retirement plan contributions for both spouses maximizing household retirement savings
Spouse can participate in accountable reimbursement plans, group health insurance, and other employee benefits

spousal_employment_benefits.business_deduction: Business deducts wages and employee benefits as ordinary and necessary expenses
spousal_employment_benefits.medical_reimbursement: Spouse employee enables §105 medical reimbursement plan covering entire family tax-free
spousal_employment_benefits.retirement_plans: Qualified plans covering spouse enable significant retirement contributions for household
spousal_employment_benefits.group_insurance: Spouse as employee enables group health insurance deduction and employee benefit programs
spousal_employment_benefits.fica_election: Sole proprietor employing only spouse can elect out of FICA (file Form 1040, Schedule H exemption)
spousal_employment_benefits.income_splitting: Shifts income recognition but household income same; benefit from marginal rate management

restrictions: Must have legitimate employer-employee relationship; spouse cannot be partner or co-owner
Compensation must be reasonable for services performed; excessive wages may be challenged
FICA exemption only available if sole proprietor employs only spouse (no other employees)
S Corporation 2%+ shareholder rules affect fringe benefits for spouse of shareholder
Must maintain documentation proving actual services performed and business necessity
Spousal wages subject to income tax withholding and must be reported on W-2
Community property states have special considerations affecting income allocation and characterization
IRS scrutinizes family employment arrangements; must demonstrate legitimate business purpose and actual work
